schema_json.description:
LLM contract for the agentic search loop 'Reason' step.

After each round of searches, the LLM analyzes results and decides
whether to continue searching or converge. Generates targeted
follow-up queries informed by what was found so far.

key_findings.description: 3-5 key findings from the latest round
key_findings.title: Key Findings

perspective_gaps.description: Underrepresented STORM perspectives in current results
perspective_gaps.title: Perspective Gaps

web_queries.description: 3-6 web queries for the next round
web_queries.title: Web Queries

academic_queries.description: 1-3 Semantic Scholar queries using precise terminology
academic_queries.title: Academic Queries

exa_queries.description: 0-1 semantic queries for Exa neural search
exa_queries.title: Exa Queries

should_continue.description: Whether another search round is needed
should_continue.title: Should Continue

reasoning.description: Brief rationale for the convergence assessment
reasoning.title: Reasoning

knowledge_gaps.description: Specific knowledge gaps identified from reading content
knowledge_gaps.title: Knowledge Gaps

schema_json.title: AgenticRoundAnalysis

prompt:
You are a research search strategist conducting an agentic deep research loop.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

ROUND HISTORY:
Round 1: 1 queries -> 10 web + 10 academic, 18 new URLs, 6 pages read
Round 2: 0 queries -> 0 web + 0 academic, 0 new URLs

KNOWLEDGE ACQUIRED (6 pages read so far):
[Page 1] Time-restricted eating for metabolic syndrome — NIH Research Matters
  Summary: NIH Research Matters (October 22, 2024) reports a Panda/Taub-led trial on time-restricted eating in people with both metabolic syndrome and prediabetes, including those on medication. The 8-10 hour eating window produced modest metabolic improvements after three months, including weight loss and better blood glucose control. The authors caution that longer-term studies are needed to understand potential benefits and drawbacks. Prior trials had mostly focused on people with obesity but without ad
  Perspectives: Scientific, Public_Health
  Key facts: Panda (Salk Institute) + Taub (UCSD) led the TRE + MetS + prediabetes trial, results published October 2024.; 8-10 hour daily eating window with no calorie counting over approximately 3 months.; Modest weight loss and better control of blood glucose reported in the MetS/prediabetes cohort.
[Page 2] PMC9946909 — IF and metabolic syndrome review/trial
  Summary: Peer-reviewed source on intermittent fasting and metabolic syndrome. The article is indexed on PubMed Central with identifier PMC9946909. Content is consistent with the broader IF/MetS literature showing short-term metabolic benefits. Substantive body text not fully captured in the fetched snippet — further extraction requires full-text reading.
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: Peer-reviewed PMC-indexed article (PMC9946909) on intermittent fasting and metabolic syndrome.; Part of the established peer-reviewed evidence base on IF in MetS populations.; Content focuses on clinical effect sizes and methodological considerations in IF trials.
[Page 3] ScienceDirect IF/MetS research article
  Summary: ScienceDirect-hosted article on intermittent fasting and cardiometabolic outcomes. Content covers pooled analyses, subgroup examinations, and GRADE quality assessment. The study reports that meta-analyses examining comorbid diabetes and specific fasting modalities found no statistically significant difference in weight loss outcomes between IF and caloric-restriction groups (MD = -1.04 kg, 95% CI -2.43 to 0.34). The Egger test was not statistically significant (P = 0.861), indicating no notable 
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: Pooled IF vs CR weight loss: mean difference -1.04 kg (95% CI -2.43 to 0.34) — not statistically significant.; Egger test for publication bias: P = 0.861 (no significant bias detected).; GRADE rating: medium quality in subgroups where total calories showed no significant difference.
[Page 4] News-Medical.net: TRE offers health benefits to adults with metabolic syndrome
  Summary: News-Medical.net news article (October 1, 2024) summarizing the Panda/Taub TRE-in-MetS trial. Reports modest benefits over three months in adults with metabolic syndrome who followed an 8-10 hour eating window. Secondary coverage of the same primary source reported by NIH.
  Perspectives: Public_Health, Scientific
  Key facts: News-Medical.net article dated October 1, 2024.; Covers the Panda/Taub TRE trial in adults with metabolic syndrome.; Reports modest metabolic benefits from an 8-10 hour eating window over approximately 3 months.
[Page 5] JAMA Network Open full article (2787246)
  Summary: JAMA Network Open full-text article on intermittent fasting or related dietary intervention. Peer-reviewed JAMA-tier publication. Content details for the fetched snippet may require full-text reading. Represents a top-tier journal contribution to the IF evidence base.
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: JAMA Network Open peer-reviewed full-text article, identifier 2787246.; Top-tier journal publication on intermittent fasting or related dietary intervention.; Part of the established peer-reviewed IF clinical trial evidence base.
[Page 6] BMC Endocrine Disorders 2025 IF/MetS article
  Summary: BMC Endocrine Disorders (Springer) 2025 article on intermittent fasting effects relevant to metabolic syndrome. Peer-reviewed article with a detailed pooled analysis across diabetic and non-diabetic subgroups. Includes Egger test, GRADE evaluation, and specific weight-loss effect size comparisons vs calorie restriction. The overall weight-loss mean difference between IF and CR was -1.04 kg (95% CI -2.43, 0.34), not statistically significant, with medium GRADE confidence.
  Perspectives: Scientific, Methodological
  Key facts: BMC Endocrine Disorders 2025 (doi 10.1186/s12902-025-01952-x) published peer-reviewed article.; Pooled IF vs CR weight-loss mean difference -1.04 kg (95% CI -2.43, 0.34, not significant).; Egger test P = 0.861 — no publication bias detected.

PERSPECTIVE COVERAGE:
  Covered (3/9): Scientific, Public_Health, Methodological
  Uncovered: Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Historical, Regional, Emerging_Trends
  Low coverage: none

Based on what has been READ:
1. Decompose the research question into 5-8 sub-questions.
   Which sub-questions have ZERO or WEAK evidence?
   Which sub-questions are well-answered with strong evidence?
2. What CONTRADICTIONS exist between sources? Flag conflicting evidence.
3. What specific KNOWLEDGE GAPS remain? List them as specific factual questions
   we CANNOT yet answer. 'none' is almost never correct for a complex research question.
4. Rate overall confidence in accumulated knowledge: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH with reasoning.
5. Which perspectives need more coverage?
6. Generate 3-6 targeted WEB queries to fill those gaps.
7. Generate 1-3 ACADEMIC queries using precise terminology.
8. Optionally generate 0-1 semantic EXA queries.
9. Assess ANSWER COMPLETENESS for the research question:
   - List specific sub-questions that remain UNANSWERED.
   - For each answered sub-question, rate evidence strength (weak/moderate/strong).
   - 'expanding': Major sub-questions still unanswered.
   - 'narrowing': Most sub-questions answered, seeking confirmation/depth.
   - 'saturated': All sub-questions answered with strong evidence.
10. Should we continue searching?

Focus on queries that address content-level gaps, not just missing URLs.
